1. לפני כל התקנת סקיל/הוק — /install-review חובה (אם לא קיים — בדיקה ידנית + אישור מפורש)
2. אסור לגשת ל-Google/GWS ללא אישור מפורש
3. לא לעשות commit/push ללא בקשה מפורשת
4. הסברים תמיד בעברית"}'

content.text:
רציתי לשנות את שם התקיה הראשית ב אקפלורר של אנטיגרבתי. 
אמרת לי לסגור את האנטיגרבטי ולשנות את שם התיקיה ואז לחזור לאנטיגרביט
כשחזרתי זה לא קיבל את השם החדש . אז העלתי מחדש את התקיה עם השם החדש

תבדוק שהכל בסדר שלא נשבר איזה קישור  כי הסורס קונטרול מראה 52 אירועים ומבקש אאאשר קומיט

addedBlocks:
## claude.ai Microsoft Learn
# Microsoft Learn MCP Server

This server gives structured access to official Microsoft and Azure documentation via three tools:

## Tools

### microsoft_docs_search
Search official documentation and return up to 10 concise, high-quality content chunks (max 500 tokens each), including title, URL, and excerpt.

- Use first to get a quick, reliable overview
- Ideal for grounding answers in Microsoft knowledge

### microsoft_code_sample_search
Search for code snippets and examples in official Microsoft Learn documentation and return up to 20 relevant, high-quality code samples.

- Use when you need to provide sample Microsoft/Azure related code in your answers.  
- Ideal for generating code snippets or practical implementation examples.  
- Optional parameter `language` can filter results. 

### microsoft_docs_fetch
Fetch and convert full Microsoft documentation pages to markdown.

- Use after search when you need full content from a specific URL
- Required for detailed tutorials, troubleshooting, prerequisites, code samples, or when search results are incomplete or outdated

## Workflow

1. Use `microsoft_docs_search` to find relevant documents.
2. If you need code examples or practical snippets, use `microsoft_code_sample_search`.
3. If deeper or complete information is needed, use `microsoft_docs_fetch`.

**Search gives breadth. Code Sample Search gives practical examples. Fetch gives depth.**

All content comes from Microsoft Learn or official sources, returned in clean markdown format.

- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

content.content:
# עיצוב חזית הדבקה על זכוכיות גוניור

[One-line description of the project goal and target audience]

---

## Tech Stack

- Framework: [e.g., Next.js 15, React 19, TypeScript]
- Styling: [e.g., Tailwind CSS, CSS Modules]
- Backend: [e.g., Node.js, PostgreSQL, Prisma]
- Node version: [e.g., 20.x]
- Package manager: [npm / pnpm / yarn]

---

## Key Commands

```
npm run dev          — start dev server
npm run build        — production build
npm test             — run test suite
npm run lint         — lint
npm run typecheck    — TypeScript check (run after every change)
```

---

## Project Structure

```
src/
  components/    — reusable UI components
  pages/         — route-level pages
  lib/           — utilities and shared logic
  styles/        — global styles and design tokens
public/          — static assets
```

---

## Core Files

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `src/lib/api.ts` | Central API client |
| `src/components/Layout.tsx` | Root layout and nav |
| `.env.example` | All required env vars (copy to `.env.local`) |

---

## Non-Negotiables

- Never commit secrets, API keys, or `.env` files.
- Never skip typechecking — run `npm run typecheck` after every non-trivial change.
- Never add a dependency without checking bundle impact first.
- No `console.log` in committed code.
- `main` is protected — work on feature branches, open a PR for review.

---

## Architecture Notes

- [Describe high-level data flow]
- [Note hard constraints]
- [Call out seams that are easy to break]

---

## Known Quirks

- [Surprising behaviors — high-value institutional memory]

---

@../rules/code-style.md
@../rules/frontend.md
@../rules/testing.md
@../rules/git-workflow.md
@../rules/performance.md
@../rules/prompt-engineering.md

# Code Style

## TypeScript

- Enable strict mode (`"strict": true` in tsconfig). No exceptions.
- No `any` — use `unknown` and narrow, or define a proper type.
- Prefer `type` over `interface` for shapes that won't be extended; use `interface` for public APIs that consumers may augment.
- Never use non-null assertion (`!`) on values you haven't verified are non-null.
- Prefer explicit return types on exported functions; infer for internal/trivial cases.

## Naming

- Files: `PascalCase` for components (`UserCard.tsx`), `camelCase` for utilities (`formatDate.ts`), `kebab-case` for route pages (`user-profile.tsx` in Next.js App Router).
- Variables/functions: `camelCase`.
- Constants and env config values: `SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE`.
- Boolean variables/props: prefix with `is`, `has`, `can`, `should` (`isLoading`, `hasError`).
- Event handlers: prefix with `handle` (`handleSubmit`, `handleChange`).

## Imports

Order (separated by blank lines, no mixing):
1. Node built-ins (`node:path`, `node:fs`)
2. External packages (`react`, `next`, third-party libs)
3. Internal aliases (`@/lib/...`, `@/components/...`)
4. Relative imports (`../utils`, `./types`)

Use named exports everywhere. No default exports — they hurt refactoring and auto-import.

## Functions

- Small, single-purpose. If a function needs more than ~30 lines, look for an extraction.
- Prefer pure functions. Side effects should be at the boundary (event handlers, effects, API calls).
- No magic numbers — name them as constants.

## Comments

- Only for non-obvious *why*, never for *what*.
- No commented-out code — delete it, git has history.
- No TODO comments in committed code unless paired with a ticket reference.

## Formatting

- Prettier handles formatting — don't fight it.
- Max line length: 100 characters.
- Trailing commas in multi-line arrays/objects/params (Prettier default).

# Frontend

## React Components

- One component per file. File name matches the component name.
- Keep components under ~150 lines — extract subcomponents or custom hooks if larger.
- Props interface defined in the same file, just above the component.
- No prop drilling beyond 2 levels — use Context or a state manager.
- Avoid `React.FC` — use plain function declarations with typed props.

```tsx
// preferred
function UserCard({ name, email }: UserCardProps) { ... }

// avoid
const UserCard: React.FC<UserCardProps> = ({ name, email }) => { ... }
```

## Hooks

- Extract logic into custom hooks when a component has more than one `useEffect` or complex derived state.
- Custom hook files: `use` prefix, `camelCase` (`useAuthSession.ts`).
- Never call hooks conditionally or inside loops.
- `useEffect` must have a complete dependency array. If lint warns, fix the dependency — don't suppress it.
- Avoid `useEffect` for derived state — compute it inline or with `useMemo`.

## State

- Keep state as close to where it's used as possible — lift only when necessary.
- Prefer `useReducer` over multiple related `useState` calls when state transitions are complex.
- Server state (fetched data) belongs in a data-fetching library (React Query, SWR, Next.js cache) — not in `useState`.

## Accessibility

- Always use semantic HTML: `<button>` for actions, `<a>` for navigation, `<nav>`, `<main>`, `<section>` etc.
- Interactive elements must be keyboard accessible (focus ring visible, `Enter`/`Space` fire the action).
- Images must have descriptive `alt` text. Decorative images: `alt=""`.
- Form inputs must have associated `<label>` (via `htmlFor` or wrapping).
- Use `aria-label` only when visible text isn't available — prefer visible labels.
- Color contrast must meet WCAG AA (4.5:1 for text, 3:1 for UI components).

## CSS / Styling

- [Tailwind / CSS Modules / styled-components — fill in your choice and remove the others]
- No inline styles except for truly dynamic values (e.g., `style={{ width: progress + '%' }}`).
- Design tokens (colors, spacing, radii) live in one place — don't hardcode hex values.
- Mobile-first breakpoints. Start with the small viewport, add larger overrides.
- Avoid `z-index` values above 50 without documenting why in a comment.

## Images & Media

- Always use `next/image` (or framework equivalent) for optimized loading.
- Provide `width` and `height` to prevent layout shift.
- Use `loading="lazy"` for images below the fold.
- Prefer SVG for icons; use an icon component system rather than ad-hoc inline SVGs.

## Error & Loading States

Every data-dependent UI must handle three states explicitly:
1. **Loading** — skeleton, spinner, or placeholder
2. **Error** — user-friendly message + retry action where applicable
3. **Empty** — intentional empty state, not just "nothing rendered"

Never leave a component that silently renders nothing on error.

# Testing

## Framework

Use [Vitest / Jest — pick one and fill in]. Never introduce a second test runner.
Component tests: [React Testing Library]. E2E: [Playwright / Cypress — pick one].

## What to Test

- **Unit test**: pure utility functions, complex business logic, edge-case branches.
- **Component test**: user-visible behavior — what the user sees and does, not implementation details.
- **E2E test**: critical user journeys (sign-up, checkout, primary feature flow).

Do not test:
- Framework internals (React re-renders, routing internals).
- Implementation details (private functions, internal state shape).
- Code that is already covered by third-party library tests.

## Structure (AAA)

```ts
it('shows error message when login fails', async () => {
  // Arrange
  server.use(http.post('/api/login', () => HttpResponse.json({ error: 'Invalid credentials' }, { status: 401 })))

  // Act
  render(<LoginForm />)
  await userEvent.type(screen.getByLabelText('Email'), 'user@example.com')
  await userEvent.click(screen.getByRole('button', { name: 'Sign in' }))

  // Assert
  expect(screen.getByRole('alert')).toHaveTextContent('Invalid credentials')
})
```

## Conventions

- Test files: co-located with source (`UserCard.test.tsx` next to `UserCard.tsx`), or in `__tests__/`.
- Test names: describe user behavior, not function names. `'shows error when...'` not `'handleSubmit throws'`.
- One logical assertion per test. Multiple `expect` calls are fine when they verify the same behavior.
- Use `userEvent` over `fireEvent` — it simulates real browser interactions.
- Don't test styles (class names). Test visibility, text content, and ARIA attributes.
- Mock at the network boundary (MSW), not at the module level, unless unavoidable.

## Coverage

- 100% coverage is not the goal — confidence is.
- Every bug fix should come with a regression test.
- Run the full suite before opening a PR.

# Git Workflow

## Branches

- `main` is protected. Never push directly.
- Branch naming: `<type>/<short-description>` — e.g., `feat/user-avatar`, `fix/login-redirect`, `chore/update-deps`.
- Types: `feat`, `fix`, `chore`, `refactor`, `docs`, `test`.
- Delete branches after merge.

## Commits

Format: `<type>: <short imperative summary>` (max 72 chars)

```
feat: add avatar upload to profile page
fix: prevent double-submit on checkout form
chore: upgrade eslint to v9
```

- One logical change per commit.
- Present tense, imperative mood: "add" not "added", "fix" not "fixes".
- Reference issue/ticket in the body when relevant: `Closes #42`.
- Never commit: secrets, `.env`, `node_modules`, build artifacts, `console.log`.

## Pull Requests

- One concern per PR. If you're fixing a bug and you spot a refactor opportunity, open a separate PR.
- PR title follows the same format as commit messages.
- Fill in the PR description: what changed, why, and how to test it.
- All CI checks must pass before requesting review.
- Squash merge into `main` — keep the history clean.
- At least one approval required before merging.

## Code Review

- Review the diff, not just the final state.
- Suggest, don't dictate — use "consider" and "what do you think about" for non-blocking feedback.
- Blocking issues must be resolved before merge; non-blocking notes can be addressed in a follow-up.
- Don't approve PRs you haven't actually read.

# Performance

## Bundle Size

- Check bundle impact before adding any dependency: `npm run build` and inspect the output, or use `bundlephobia.com`.
- Prefer smaller focused packages over large ones (e.g., `date-fns` over `moment`, `zod` over `yup`).
- Never import an entire library when you need one function: `import { debounce } from 'lodash-es'` not `import _ from 'lodash'`.
- Tree-shaking only works with ES modules — avoid CJS-only packages where possible.

## Code Splitting

- Lazy-load route-level components: `const Page = lazy(() => import('./Page'))`.
- Heavy components (rich text editors, charts, map libraries) must be lazy-loaded.
- Never lazy-load components that appear above the fold on first load — it causes visible pop-in.

## Rendering

- Avoid unnecessary re-renders:
  - Don't create new objects/arrays/functions inline in JSX props if the child is memoized.
  - `useMemo` and `useCallback` are not free — only use them when you have a measured problem or a stable reference is required (e.g., passing to a memoized child or `useEffect` dependency).
- Avoid reading from the DOM in a render cycle (`getBoundingClientRect`, `scrollTop`) — use `useLayoutEffect` if unavoidable.
- Lists with 50+ items should be virtualized (`react-virtual`, `tanstack-virtual`).

## Images

- Use next-gen formats (WebP, AVIF).
- Always provide `width` and `height` to prevent Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS).
- Hero images: `fetchpriority="high"`, `loading="eager"`. All others: `loading="lazy"`.

## Web Vitals Targets

| Metric | Target |
|---|---|
| LCP (Largest Contentful Paint) | < 2.5s |
| CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift) | < 0.1 |
| INP (Interaction to Next Paint) | < 200ms |

Measure with Lighthouse CI or the browser DevTools Performance panel before shipping features that change the critical rendering path.

# Prompt Engineering

## Core Principles

- Be explicit about the task, context, and desired output format upfront.
- One prompt, one goal — don't bundle multiple unrelated tasks into a single prompt.
- Show, don't just tell: include examples (few-shot) when the format or behavior is non-obvious.
- Specify constraints explicitly: length, tone, language, what to avoid.

## Prompt Structure

Preferred order for complex prompts:

1. **Role / context** — who the model is or what it knows
2. **Task** — what it must do, in one sentence
3. **Input** — the data or text to work with
4. **Constraints** — format, length, things to avoid
5. **Output format** — exact structure expected (JSON, markdown, list, etc.)

```
You are a senior copywriter for a glass-design company.
Task: Write a 3-sentence product description for the item below.
Input: [product details]
Constraints: No superlatives, use second-person ("you"), max 60 words.
Output: Plain paragraph, no headers or bullets.
```

## System vs User Messages

- System message: persistent identity, behavioral rules, output format contracts.
- User message: the actual task and input data.
- Never mix identity instructions into user messages — they get overridden easily.

## Few-Shot Examples

- Use 2–3 examples when the output pattern is non-trivial.
- Examples must match the exact format you want back — inconsistent examples produce inconsistent output.
- Order matters: put the most representative example last (recency bias).

## Chaining

- Break multi-step reasoning into sequential prompts — don't ask the model to do 5 things in one shot.
- Pass only the relevant output of step N into step N+1; don't carry the full history.
- For classification → extraction → rewrite pipelines: validate the output of each step before moving on.

## Temperature & Sampling

| Use case | Temperature |
|---|---|
| Data extraction, classification | 0 – 0.2 |
| Copywriting, product descriptions | 0.5 – 0.8 |
| Brainstorming, ideation | 0.9 – 1.2 |

- Keep temperature at 0 for anything that must be deterministic (structured data, code).
- Don't raise temperature to "fix" bad prompts — fix the prompt first.

## Variables in Prompts

- Mark dynamic inputs clearly: `{{product_name}}`, `{{user_input}}`.
- Sanitize user-supplied content before interpolating — prevent prompt injection.
- Never concatenate raw user input directly into a system message.

## Evaluation

- Write an eval set (10–20 examples) before iterating on a production prompt.
- A prompt change that improves one case may regress another — always test the full set.
- Log prompt + input + output in production for offline analysis.

## Common Mistakes

- **Under-specifying format**: "Give me a list" is ambiguous — say "numbered list, one item per line".
- **Over-prompting**: Adding 10 rules bloats the prompt and the model starts ignoring some.
- **Vague negatives**: "Don't be too formal" — say "Use casual tone, as if texting a colleague".
- **No grounding**: Asking for facts without providing source material leads to hallucinations.
- **Changing two things at once**: When iterating, change one variable per test so you know what worked.

- valyu-best-practices
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

content.thinking:
The user is asking a really thoughtful question about how to create a "maximum state" where:
1. Access to all skills
2. Important rules are loaded
3. Claude continues to update and explore (like they requested for changes)
4. But remains "lean" - not burning tokens without impact on access level

This is a meta question about optimizing their Claude Code setup. Let me think about this carefully.

The key tension here is:
- **Breadth of access** (skills, rules, directories) → requires loading context
- **Token efficiency** → fewer things loaded = less cost per turn

Let me think about the architecture of how Claude Code loads context:
The real optimization is that skills don't consume tokens until they're actually invoked—having them accessible in the directory doesn't automatically load them into context. So I can grant broad access through `additionalDirectories` and skill references while keeping the baseline context lean, then load specific skills on-demand via the `/skill-name` syntax or the Skill tool.

The constant token burn comes from CLAUDE.md and its referenced `@rules/` files (loaded at session start), MEMORY.md, and any SessionStart hook output. The skills list itself in the system reminder is just names, so that's negligible. This means I should keep CLAUDE.md and rules tight and high-signal, while being generous with available skills since they're only loaded when needed.

Looking at the current setup with the SessionStart hook injecting rules, Stop hook with code review, PostToolUse for typechecking, and PreToolUse for safety checks—the key optimization is making sure CLAUDE.md stays lean while keeping those hooks focused on their specific purposes.

The Stop hook running on every turn seems wasteful if it's meant to catch code changes, so I should consider triggering it only when there's actual code to review. For the "continue to update and explore" part, that sounds like the user wants Claude to stay aware of project changes—maybe through a hook that periodically checks for modifications rather than running constantly. The typecheck hook should only trigger when TypeScript or TSX files actually change. For the broader goal of continuous tracking, I could leverage the existing Stop hook since it already reviews code changes, or enhance SessionStart to scan recent git history and brief me on what's evolved. My main recommendations are trimming those empty template sections from CLAUDE.md to save tokens, consolidating any redundant content across the rules files, and refining the Stop hook logic to be more selective about when it runs. The PreCompact hook handles context overflow, but for maintaining continuity across sessions, the memory system is the real workhorse—keeping MEMORY.md lean and focused ensures it stays useful without bloating the token budget.

content.text:
שאלה טובה. יש כאן שלוש צירי מתח שכדאי להבין:

---

## מה שורף טוקנים ומה לא

| רכיב | עלות בטוקנים | הערה |
|---|---|---|
| שמות סקילס בלבד | כמעט אפס | רק רשימת שמות ב-system reminder |
| תוכן סקיל | גבוה | **רק כשמופעל** — לא נטען בלי קריאה |
| CLAUDE.md + @rules | גבוה | נטען **בכל שיחה** |
| SessionStart hook | בינוני | מופעל פעם אחת בפתיחה |
| Stop hook (ריוי) | גבוה | מופעל בכל סיום |
| MEMORY.md | נמוך-בינוני | נטען תמיד |

**מסקנה:** סקילס הם בחינם — הבעיה היא CLAUDE.md ו-rules שנטענים תמיד.

---

## מה כדאי לשפר בהגדרה שלך

**1. CLAUDE.md — יש שם sections ריקות שמבזבזות טוקנים:**
```
## Architecture Notes
- [Describe high-level data flow]   ← טוקנים על כלום
```
אנחנו נמחק את כל ה-placeholders שלא מולאו.

**2. Rules — 6 קבצים נטענים בכל שיחה.** חלקם כוללים דברים כמו טבלאות ארוכות של Web Vitals ו-temperature ranges — אלה reference material שמישי רק לפעמים, לא ב-100% מהשיחות.

**3. Stop hook (ריוי)** — כבר חכם (בודק אם יש קוד שהשתנה), אבל כשיש שינויים הוא עולה ב-agent שלם.

---

## הגדרת "מקסימום עם רזון"

מה שאציע:

1. **נגזום את CLAUDE.md** — נמחק placeholders, נשאיר רק מה שמולא
2. **נפצל rules** — נפריד בין "תמיד טען" (non-negotiables, style) לבין "טען לפי בקשה" (performance metrics, test structure)
3. **שמירת רציפות** — לוגיקה שמכניסה ל-memory את השינויים החשובים אחרי כל session, כדי שתדע מה קרה בלי לטעון הכל

**שאלה לפני שמתחיל:** האם "להמשיך לחקור ולהתעדכן" שציינת מתייחס לדבר ספציפי שביקשת בעבר (hook, cron, משהו אחר), או הכוונה שבכל שיחה חדשה תהיה לי תמונה מעודכנת על הפרויקט?

content.text:
בסדר, אסביר בפשטות.

---

## איך Claude Code טוען מידע — בפועל

כשמתחילה שיחה, יש שני סוגי מידע:

**מידע שנטען אוטומטית בכל שיחה:**
- CLAUDE.md (ו-rules שמקושרים אליו ב-`@`)
- MEMORY.md
- פלט של SessionStart hook

**מידע שנטען רק כשצריך:**
- תוכן של סקיל — רק כשמפעילים אותו

כשיש לך 6 קבצי rules שכוללים טבלאות, דוגמאות קוד, ו-Web Vitals metrics — **כל זה נטען בכל שיחה**, גם אם אתה רק שואל שאלה פשוטה.

content.thinking:
The Stop hook ran (ריוי) and reported that no code files were changed, so no code review was needed. But the user is pointing out that we talked about implementing the session continuity feature but didn't actually implement it.

The user seems to be saying "yes, implement it" - they want the Stop hook to also save important changes/notes to memory after each session.

Let me think about what this should do:

The Stop hook should:
1. Check what happened in the session (git diff, conversation summary)
2. If there are important changes or decisions, save them to memory

But the Stop hook is an agent that runs after each session. It would need to:
- Run git commands to see what changed
- If there are meaningful changes, write a brief note to the project memory about what was done

The challenge is that the Stop hook agent doesn't have access to the conversation history, so it can only work with what git shows us.

A simpler approach would be to add a second hook that checks for commits or unstaged changes, then writes a brief session note to memory if there's something worth recording.

Writing hook configuration...

I'm reconsidering whether the session log should be part of the formal memory system with frontmatter or just a simple append-only file. The most practical approach seems to be a dedicated `session-log.md` that only gets updated when there's an actual commit, since that's the clearest signal that meaningful work happened. Let me check what memory files already exist to see if there's something similar in place.

Looking at the available memory files, there's no session log yet. For the implementation, the stop hook will need to work with what git can tell it—commits made and uncommitted changes—since it won't have access to the conversation context itself. The hook should capture the git log since the last session and any modified files, using commit messages to document the what and why.

The tricky part is determining what counts as "this session's work." Since the user already pushed their changes, comparing against origin/master won't show anything. A better approach is to check recent commits within a time window and look for any uncommitted changes, then write meaningful entries to the session log if there's something worth recording. The user is pointing out that the Stop hook ran and reported no changes, which is exactly the problem we should solve—this conversation itself should be logged. So I need to implement the session continuity feature now by adding a hook to the Stop array in settings.json and creating a session-log memory file that captures what happened during this session.

My approach is to keep it simple: the hook will check git state (recent commits, uncommitted changes, stashed work) and write a brief summary to the session log if there was meaningful activity.

Writing the hook configuration... Writing hook configuration... I see the project path uses a hash for the Hebrew characters in the project directory, so the full memory file path is `C:\Users\yaniv\.claude\projects\c--Users-yaniv-----------------------------------\memory\session-log.md`. Now I'll set up the session recorder by editing the settings file, creating the memory file, and updating the documentation.

I should go ahead and implement this since the user's feedback about "nothing was saved" indicates they want this feature. The session log should be project-specific since it tracks activity within this project, and I'll design it to auto-trim older entries to prevent unbounded growth.

Writing the hook configuration...

The user wants me to read what I wrote earlier under "הגדרת מקסימום עם רזון" and implement it. Let me recall what I suggested:

1. **גזום את CLAUDE.md** — מחק placeholders, השאר רק מה שמולא
2. **פצל rules** — הפרד בין "תמיד טען" לבין "טען לפי בקשה"
3. **שמירת רציפות** — כבר יושם (session-log hook)

So now I need to:
I'm looking at the CLAUDE.md file to see what template sections are empty and need to be removed, keeping only the parts that are actually filled in. The file has a tech stack section with several placeholders that should be cleaned up. I'm noticing that most of this template is filled with placeholder text and generic examples that don't actually match the project. The tech stack section, key commands, project structure, and architecture notes are all just boilerplate with `[...]` placeholders that waste tokens without providing real value. The core files reference paths that probably don't exist. For the rules being loaded, I should evaluate which ones are truly essential for every session versus ones that can be referenced on demand — code-style is definitely needed, but I should reconsider whether all six rules need to load automatically each time. they're asking if removing these files from automatic loading is safe and won't cause me to forget things. The key insight is that the files still exist and I can access them when needed—I'm just not loading them automatically every session. There's a real risk though: if I'm working on a React component without frontend.md loaded, I might not follow those specific rules, but I can always pull it in when necessary.

For a project still in early setup with no actual code yet, auto-loading all six rules files is wasteful. I should keep code-style.md and git-workflow.md since those are always relevant, remove the others from @references but document in CLAUDE.md that they're available on request, and reassess once the tech stack is chosen and actual development begins.

Now I'll clean up the CLAUDE.md file by removing empty template sections like the generic Tech Stack, Key Commands, and Project Structure placeholders, keeping only the Non-Negotiables section and the two essential rule file references. I need to actually implement what I outlined earlier. I've already trimmed the empty sections, so now I'm focusing on splitting the rules into ones that should always load versus ones available on demand—keeping code-style and git-workflow as references while marking the others as optional. Let me review the current CLAUDE.md to see what I'm working with.

21	npm run build        — production build

30

53

# עיצוב חזית הדבקה על זכוכיות גוניור

[One-line description of the project goal and target audience]

---

## Tech Stack

- Framework: [e.g., Next.js 15, React 19, TypeScript]
- Styling: [e.g., Tailwind CSS, CSS Modules]
- Backend: [e.g., Node.js, PostgreSQL, Prisma]
- Node version: [e.g., 20.x]
- Package manager: [npm / pnpm / yarn]

---

## Key Commands

```
npm run dev          — start dev server
npm run build        — production build
npm test             — run test suite
npm run lint         — lint
npm run typecheck    — TypeScript check (run after every change)
```

---

## Project Structure

```
src/
  components/    — reusable UI components
  pages/         — route-level pages
  lib/           — utilities and shared logic
  styles/        — global styles and design tokens
public/          — static assets
```

---

## Core Files

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `src/lib/api.ts` | Central API client |
| `src/components/Layout.tsx` | Root layout and nav |
| `.env.example` | All required env vars (copy to `.env.local`) |

---

## Non-Negotiables

- Never commit secrets, API keys, or `.env` files.
- Never skip typechecking — run `npm run typecheck` after every non-trivial change.
- Never add a dependency without checking bundle impact first.
- No `console.log` in committed code.
- `main` is protected — work on feature branches, open a PR for review.

---

## Architecture Notes

- [Describe high-level data flow]

content.thinking:
The user is asking two questions:
1. What's the perfect way to set up security, rules, agents, memory to get maximum benefit but work efficiently?
2. If they have a new task, should they continue in the same chat or open a new folder, and if a new folder, where?

Let me answer both questions clearly and concisely.

**Question 1: Perfect architecture for security/rules/agents/memory**

This is about Claude Code's architecture. The key components are:
- **Security**: hooks that block destructive commands, install-review before installing skills
- **Rules**: CLAUDE.md + rules/ files (lean, always-loaded vs on-demand)
- **Agents**: Stop hooks for code review and session recording, scheduled agents like Saturday research runs
- **Memory**: MEMORY.md as an index with individual memory files loaded on demand

The real pattern here is organizing by scope and load strategy. Global CLAUDE.md holds identity and non-negotiables, project-level CLAUDE.md keeps things lean and specific, memory gets indexed but files are read when needed, skills only activate when called, and hooks respond to events. This layered approach lets you have comprehensive rules without bloating every interaction.

For security, I'm using a PreToolUse hook to block destructive bash commands, an install-review rule in the global CLAUDE.md, and SessionStart reminders about critical rules. On the practical side, staying in the same chat works for the same project, but switching to a different folder and Claude Code session makes sense when moving to a completely different project.

content.text:
שתי שאלות נפרדות — אענה על כל אחת:

---

## 1. ארכיטקטורה מושלמת

המודל הנכון הוא **שכבות לפי תדירות שימוש:**

```
תמיד נטען          → Global CLAUDE.md (זהות + non-negotiables)
                    → Project CLAUDE.md (lean — רק מה שספציפי לפרויקט)
                    → MEMORY.md (אינדקס בלבד)

בעת אירוע          → Hooks (Stop/PreToolUse/SessionStart)

לפי דרישה          → Rules files (reference material)
                    → Memory files (נקראים כשרלוונטיים)
                    → Skills (נטענים רק כשמופעלים)
```

**אבטחה:** שלוש שכבות:
- PreToolUse חוסם פקודות הרסניות
- install-review לפני כל התקנה
- SessionStart מזכיר את הכללים הקריטיים

**מה שיש לך עכשיו** — כבר קרוב מאוד לזה. הדבר היחיד שחסר הוא למלא את CLAUDE.md עם ה-tech stack האמיתי ברגע שיוחלט.

---

## 2. אותו צ'אט או תיקיה חדשה?

| מצב | מה עושים |
|---|---|
| משימה חדשה **באותו פרויקט** | שיחה חדשה באותה תיקיה (`ליו`) |
| פרויקט אחר לגמרי | תיקיה חדשה תחת `C:\Users\yaniv\` |

**שיחה חדשה** = context נקי, אבל אותו settings.json + CLAUDE.md + memory. זה מספיק לרוב המשימות.

**תיקיה חדשה** = רק אם זה קודבייס אחר, מטרה אחרת, rules אחרים.

---

אז לשאלה שלך — אם המשימה החדשה היא עדיין על פרויקט גוניור: שיחה חדשה באותה תיקיה. מה המשימה?

So opening a new folder means losing the ליו-specific configurations, rules, and memory, but keeping everything in the global Claude directory and any global skills. the global skills are always available, but project-specific skills, rules, and settings only apply within that project. So opening a new folder would give them access to all the global skills and settings, but they'd lose the ליו project's custom skills, documentation, and hooks.

content.text: מה אתה חושב שבכהכי נכון תסביר לי

content.thinking:
The user is asking me what I think is the most correct approach. Let me think about this carefully.

The question is: should the hooks (ריוי, session recorder) be global or project-specific?

Let me think about each component:

**ריוי (code review on Stop):**
- Makes sense globally — you want code review in every project
- But the prompt references specific file types and behaviors — those are universal
- Should be global

**Session recorder:**
- This writes to a specific project memory path: `C:\Users\yaniv\.claude\projects\c--Users-yaniv-----------------------------------\memory\session-log.md`
- That path is project-specific, so putting it globally would write to the same file across all projects, which might actually be fine as a unified log
- But the memory system itself is per-project based on the directory hash, and the hook has a hardcoded path to the ליו project
- For it to work globally, the agent would need to dynamically determine the correct memory path for each project — that's more complex

**Beep, PreToolUse, and SessionStart reminder:**
- Beep and the destructive command blocker are universal and should be global
- The SessionStart reminder is currently project-specific with Hebrew rules, but could move to global if the content applies universally

**For the session recorder specifically:**
- The memory path depends on the project's directory hash, so it needs to know which project it's running in
- The agent could figure this out dynamically by checking the current working directory or environment variables and computing the memory path on the fly
- This would let the hook work across projects without hardcoding paths

**The cleaner split is:**
- Global hooks handle what applies everywhere: the beep on stop, destructive command blocking, code review, and universal session reminders
- Project-specific hooks handle what varies: the session recorder with its dynamic memory path, typecheck commands that depend on the tech stack, and any project-specific session setup
- Need to avoid duplication if the code review agent ends up in both global and local settings

**The real question is:** what should consistently happen in every coding session regardless of which project I'm in? The simpler path is to have the agent write session logs to a local file like `.claude/session-log.md` in the project directory, though that risks committing it to git. I could also have it compute the memory directory path dynamically using the project root, but that's getting complicated. Let me step back and give a straightforward recommendation instead: move beep, the destructive command blocker, and code review to global settings since they're universal, while keeping the TypeScript typecheck project-specific since it depends on the project's setup.

For new projects, running `/init` would copy the session recorder and typecheck hooks from existing settings, and since the path is relative to each project's unique hash, it would automatically write to the right location. But I'm realizing the hardcoded path in my session recorder is specific to this project's hash—a different project would need a different path entirely. So the session recorder either stays project-specific with each project getting its own hook, or I need to make it smart enough to figure out the right path dynamically.

I think the cleaner approach is a three-tier architecture: global hooks that work everywhere (beep, the PreToolUse blocker, and the Hebrew reminders), a project template tier with the session recorder and typecheck hooks that gets copied to each new project, and then project-specific content like CLAUDE.md and memory files. I could keep a template in `~/.claude/templates/project/` with the standard hooks ready to copy.

The global hooks like the beep, code review, destructive command blocker, and quality reviewers will work anywhere, but the project-specific ones won't—which is why a template approach in ~/.claude/templates/ would be useful for bootstrapping new projects.

content.thinking:
The user is asking if there's a command that automatically applies the template when opening a new project, rather than manually copying files.

In Claude Code, there's `/init` which initializes a new project. But I'm not sure if it uses the template directory automatically.

The truth is: Claude Code's `/init` command creates a CLAUDE.md based on the project, but I don't think it automatically copies from `~/.claude/templates/project/`. The templates directory might be used by specific skills or commands.

Let me think about what options exist:

1. `/init` - creates a CLAUDE.md, might not use the template
2. A custom skill or script that handles the copying
3. A SessionStart hook in global settings that could detect new projects and apply the template automatically

There's no built-in automatic mechanism that copies from the templates directory when starting a new project. The `/init` command generates a CLAUDE.md but doesn't pull from the template. I could work around this by creating a custom skill for template initialization, or by setting up a SessionStart hook that checks if a project is new and copies the template files accordingly.

The cleanest approach would be a global SessionStart hook that detects when a project lacks a settings.json and automatically copies from the template directory. Though I'd need to be careful about gating this so it doesn't copy to system folders or projects where we don't want it. Alternatively, I could create a dedicated `/setup-project` skill that users invoke once when initializing a new project.

content.thinking:
The user wants:
1. A summary of all skills and rules that could be "lost" when opening a new project (i.e., what's project-specific vs global)
2. How to start a new project with full capabilities

Let me think about this comprehensively.

**What's GLOBAL (never lost, always available everywhere):**
- Global CLAUDE.md (`C:\Users\yaniv\CLAUDE.md`) - non-negotiables, workflow, coding style
- Global settings.json hooks: Beep, ריוי, destructive command blocker, code quality reviewer, backup before overwrite
- Global SessionStart hook (new): auto-copies template to new projects
- All 500+ system skills are always accessible, plus the Shannon and valyu-best-practices skills which are symlinked into the ליו project folder

**What's PROJECT-SPECIFIC for ליו (could be "lost" in new project):**
- Project CLAUDE.md with project-specific non-negotiables and @rules references
- Rules files like code-style.md and git-workflow.md, plus on-demand files for frontend, testing, performance, and prompt engineering
- Project settings.json hooks for SessionStart reminders, session recording, type checking, and PreComp
- Memory files (MEMORY.md and individual memory files)
- Saturday cron/schedule for deep research

With the new auto-copy hook, fresh projects will automatically get the SessionStart reminder, session recorder, TypeScript type checking, and PreComp from the template, but they won't inherit the project-specific CLAUDE.md, rules files, memory, specialized skills, or the Saturday cron—those need to be set up fresh for each project.

For capabilities like web access and APIs, I'm checking what's actually enabled in the project settings. WebSearch and WebFetch are in the allow lists, and the project has MCPs configured for Playwright browser automation, Gmail, Microsoft Learn, and Google Drive—but these are all project-specific configurations in `.mcp.json`, so a new project would need those MCPs reconfigured separately.

To set up a new project, the workflow is straightforward: create a folder, initialize git (which triggers the auto-copy hook for settings), open it in Claude Code, and then add any MCPs or rules files as needed. Let me verify what's actually in the current `.mcp.json` to give you the exact details.

content.text:
נחלק לשלוש קטגוריות:

---

## מה שקיים תמיד (גלובלי — לא נאבד לעולם)

**Hooks:**
- Beep בסיום
- ריוי (code review)
- חוסם פקודות הרסניות
- Code quality reviewer (מסיר console.log, any types וכו')
- Backup לפני כתיבה/מחיקה
- Auto-copy template לפרויקט חדש ✓ (חדש)

**Skills:**
כל 500+ הסקילס ברשימת המערכת — תמיד נגישים בכל פרויקט

**כללים:**
כל מה שב-`C:\Users\yaniv\CLAUDE.md` — זהות, workflow, non-negotiables, guardrails

---

## מה שנאבד בפרויקט חדש (ויתוקן אוטומטית עכשיו)

| רכיב | מה קורה |
|---|---|
| SessionStart reminder | ✓ מועתק מ-template |
| Session recorder | ✓ מועתק מ-template |
| TypeScript typecheck | ✓ מועתק מ-template |
| PreCompact reminder | ✓ מועתק מ-template |

---

## מה שנאבד ודורש הגדרה ידנית

Actually, looking at Claude Code documentation I know:

Adding more MCPs...

I'm thinking the global CLAUDE.md is the best place for this behavior rule since it applies across all sessions. I'm realizing that Claude Code supports global MCPs through a command-line approach, so instead of copying files to each project template, I should configure MCPs globally at the user level—this way they're available everywhere without needing file duplication. I'll suggest adding the permission rule to the global CLAUDE.md to ensure Claude asks before establishing any external connections. # CLAUDE.md

## Identity

You are a pragmatic, senior-level engineering assistant. Favor clarity and correctness over cleverness. When in doubt, ask — don't assume.

---

## Non-Negotiables

- Never modify files outside the current task scope without asking first.
- Never commit or push without explicit user instruction.
- Never skip error handling at system boundaries (user input, external APIs).
- Never add comments that explain *what* the code does — only add comments for non-obvious *why*.
- Never create README or documentation files unless explicitly asked.
- Never add features, abstractions, or refactors beyond what was asked.
- If a task is ambiguous, ask clarifying questions before starting.

---

## Environment

- **OS:** Windows 11, shell: PowerShell (primary), Bash available via WSL/Git Bash.
- **IDE:** Antigravity IDE (VS Code fork) — extensions live in `~\.antigravity-ide\extensions\`.
- **Home dir:** `C:\Users\yaniv` — no project source here; navigate to relevant subdirectory first.
- Use PowerShell syntax by default (`$env:VAR`, backtick line continuation, no `&&` operator).

---

## Coding Style

# CLAUDE.md

## Identity

You are a pragmatic, senior-level engineering assistant. Favor clarity and correctness over cleverness. When in doubt, ask — don't assume.

---

## Non-Negotiables

- Never modify files outside the current task scope without asking first.
- Never commit or push without explicit user instruction.
- Never skip error handling at system boundaries (user input, external APIs).
- Never add comments that explain *what* the code does — only add comments for non-obvious *why*.
- Never create README or documentation files unless explicitly asked.
- Never add features, abstractions, or refactors beyond what was asked.
- If a task is ambiguous, ask clarifying questions before starting.

---

## Environment

- **OS:** Windows 11, shell: PowerShell (primary), Bash available via WSL/Git Bash.
- **IDE:** Antigravity IDE (VS Code fork) — extensions live in `~\.antigravity-ide\extensions\`.
- **Home dir:** `C:\Users\yaniv` — no project source here; navigate to relevant subdirectory first.
- Use PowerShell syntax by default (`$env:VAR`, backtick line continuation, no `&&` operator).

---

## Coding Style

- Readability > cleverness.
- Small, focused functions with clear names.
- Prefer editing existing files over creating new ones.
- No half-finished implementations — complete what you start or say so explicitly.
- No backwards-compatibility shims for removed code.
- Three similar lines is better than a premature abstraction.

---

## Workflow

1. **Plan before coding** — for any non-trivial task, use `/plan` or state your approach and get explicit approval before writing a single line. Never jump straight to implementation.
2. **Understand first** — read relevant files before editing.
3. **Break tasks small** — one logical change at a time. If it feels large, split it and confirm scope.
4. **Verify before reporting done** — run tests, inspect the diff, confirm the golden path works.
5. **Stay scoped** — a bug fix doesn't need surrounding cleanup.

---

## High-Stakes Guardrails

Require explicit user confirmation before any of these — do not proceed autonomously:

- Architecture decisions or major structural refactors
- Security-relevant changes (auth, permissions, secrets, data handling)
- Production config, CI/CD pipeline, or infrastructure changes
- Anything irreversible: deleting data, dropping tables, force-pushing, removing branches

## Skill & Hook Installation

**Before installing any skill or hook from any source, always invoke `/install-review` first.**

- Do not copy files to `~/.claude/skills/` or add hooks to `settings.json` until the review completes.
- If the review flags ⚠️ REVIEW REQUIRED or 🚫 DO NOT INSTALL on any item, stop and wait for explicit user approval before proceeding.
- This applies to: GitHub repos, pasted skill content, hook scripts, `.md` files, `.py`/`.sh` files.

---

## Context & Self-Improvement

- Use `/compact` when context grows large; before compacting, summarize open decisions and TODOs.
- After every correction or unexpected behavior: turn it into a rule in `CLAUDE.md` or a `rules/` file.
- Keep `CLAUDE.md` and `rules/` short and high-signal — remove stale entries.
- After a non-trivial session, note any patterns that kept causing rework.

---

## Common Pitfalls to Avoid

- Providing explanations when code changes were asked for.
- Adding trailing summaries of what you just did ("I have now updated...").
- Inventing URLs or package names — verify they exist.
- Using `rm -rf`, `git reset --hard`, or other destructive commands without confirmation.
- Over-engineering for hypothetical future requirements.

---

## Project Navigation

Each project has its own subdirectory under `C:\Users\yaniv\`. Check for a subfolder `CLAUDE.md` when entering a project — it overrides or extends these global rules.

> **Note:** Any index files referenced in subprojects may be outdated — always verify by exploring the actual files.

lines: - Never modify files outside the current task scope without asking first.
- Never commit or push without explicit user instruction.
+- Never use external connections (browser/Playwright, GitHub API, web search, any MCP tool) without stating the intent and receiving explicit permission first.
- Never skip error handling at system boundaries (user input, external APIs).
- Never add comments that explain *what* the code does — only add comments for non-obvious *why*.
- Never create README or documentation files unless explicitly asked.

blockingError.command:
You are a strict code quality reviewer. A file was just written or edited.

Event data: $ARGUMENTS

Instructions:
1. Parse the JSON. Get the file path from tool_input.file_path.
2. Check the file extension. ONLY process these: .ts .tsx .js .jsx .py .go .rs .c .cpp .h .css .scss .java — skip everything else (JSON, markdown, config, lock files, images) and exit immediately.
3. Read the file content.
4. Scan for these issues and AUTO-FIX them if found:
   a. console.log / console.error / console.warn calls — remove them entirely
   b. Blocks of commented-out code (not explanatory comments, just dead code) — delete them
   c. TypeScript `any` type — replace with `unknown` or a proper type
   d. Obvious code duplication (3+ identical lines) — extract to a variable or helper
5. For these issues, DO NOT auto-fix — add a comment ONLY if there is no comment already:
   - Functions longer than ~30 lines (note: consider extracting)
   - Magic numbers used in logic (note: consider naming as a constant)
6. After each Edit you make, re-read the file to verify the fix looks correct and didn't introduce new issues.
7. Repeat the scan-and-fix loop until a full scan finds no more auto-fixable issues.
8. If you made no changes, do nothing and return silently.

For PostToolUse hooks, blocking might work differently than PreToolUse. I'm considering a few approaches: adding a file extension matcher to the hook itself, creating a pre-check command hook that validates the file type before the agent runs, or refining the agent's instructions to produce absolutely no output when skipping non-code files. The core issue seems to be that the agent outputs a blocking message even when it should be silent, so making the skip behavior truly silent might be the cleanest solution. The real solution is to add a pre-check command hook that runs before the agent hook, examining the file extension from the arguments and skipping the entire chain if it's not a code file. Writing the JSON configuration... The PostToolUse hook system doesn't actually prevent subsequent hooks from running in the same array, so I need a different approach. I'm wondering if the matcher field in Claude Code's PostToolUse hook can actually filter based on file paths using regex—specifically matching code file extensions like `.ts`, `.js`, `.py`, etc. I'm not entirely certain if the matcher works that way or if it only matches against tool names, so I need to verify this against the actual Claude Code documentation. The pragmatic approach is to accept this as a known limitation and focus on what's actually useful: the global PostToolUse reviewer does valuable real-time fixes for console.log and type issues, so I'll keep it but make the agent prompt more explicit about silently skipping non-code files to reduce noise on JSON and other file types. I'm realizing the PostToolUse hook might be outputting something even when the agent skips, which the framework then reports as activity. input.description: Research Claude Code configuration best practices
input.prompt:
Research the current best practices for Claude Code configuration. I need a concise comparison report (under 400 words) covering:

1. CLAUDE.md best practices — what should be in global vs project-level CLAUDE.md, recommended structure, what to avoid
2. Hooks — recommended hook patterns (SessionStart, Stop, PreToolUse, PostToolUse, PreCompact), any pitfalls
3. Memory system — how it works, best practices for memory files
4. MCP configuration — global vs project-level MCPs, recommended setup
5. Skills — how they load, any best practices

Focus on: what the official docs or community recommends vs common mistakes. Keep it factual and concise.

content.text:
Perfect. Now I have comprehensive information from the official documentation. Let me compile a concise comparison report.

---

## Claude Code Configuration Best Practices Report

### 1. CLAUDE.md Files

**Official Best Practice:**
- **Global scope** (`~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`): Personal preferences, coding style, personal tools
- **Project scope** (`.claude/CLAUDE.md`): Team-shared guidelines, architecture, build commands, coding standards
- **Local scope** (`.claude.local.md`): Sandbox URLs, personal test data; gitignored
- **Managed** (admin-deployed): Organization policies, compliance, security

**Size & Structure:**
- Target under 200 lines per file — longer files consume context and reduce adherence
- Use markdown headers and path-scoped `.claude/rules/` files for large projects
- Specific, concrete instructions work best ("use 2-space indentation" vs "format properly")

**Common Mistakes:**
- Mixing personal preferences into project CLAUDE.md (pollutes shared context)
- Storing large reference material inline instead of using `@path` imports or skills
- Conflicting instructions across multiple CLAUDE.md files
- Vague rules that are hard to verify

---

### 2. Hooks

**Recommended Hook Patterns:**
- **SessionStart**: Load development context (branch, uncommitted changes, open issues) — keep fast
- **PreToolUse**: Block dangerous operations (rm -rf, destructive commands) by inspecting input before execution
- **PostToolUse**: Run validation after file writes (linting, type-checking)
- **Stop**: Provide feedback or continue conversation (can block to prevent stopping)

**Hook Configuration Hierarchy** (precedence order):
1. Managed policy settings (admin-controlled)
2. `.claude/settings.local.json` (project-specific, gitignored)
3. `.claude/settings.json` (project-shared)
4. `~/.claude/settings.json` (all projects)

**Pitfalls:**
- Long-running hooks slow down every session start
- Using hooks for guidance instead of enforcement (CLAUDE.md is better for behavioral rules)
- Conflicting matchers across hook files
- Exit code confusion: exit 0 = no decision; exit 2 = blocking error

---

### 3. Memory System

**Architecture:**
- **CLAUDE.md**: Instructions you write, loaded in full at session start, survives compaction
- **Auto memory**: Claude-written notes saved to `~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/MEMORY.md`
- **Auto memory loading**: First 200 lines or 25KB of `MEMORY.md` loaded per session; topic files loaded on-demand
- **Scope**: Per-project (all worktrees + subdirectories of same repo share one memory directory)

**Best Practices:**
- Use CLAUDE.md for facts, standards, architecture that don't change
- Let auto memory accumulate learnings (build commands, debugging patterns, preferences)
- Keep CLAUDE.md concise — auto memory handles growing context naturally
- Use `/memory` command to audit what's stored

**Common Mistakes:**
- Putting procedural instructions in CLAUDE.md instead of skills (skills load on-demand)
- Assuming auto memory syncs across machines (it doesn't — local only)
- Treating CLAUDE.md as "enforced" (it's context, not enforcement — use hooks for blocking)

---

### 4. MCP Configuration

**Scope:**
- **User scope** (`~/.claude.json`): Personal integrations, tools you use across projects
- **Project scope** (`.mcp.json`): Team-required servers (GitHub, memory, monitoring)
- **Managed settings**: Organization allowlists/denylists

**Best Practices:**
- Keep `.mcp.json` in version control for team consistency
- Use allowlists (`allowedMcpServers`) to restrict which servers developers can add
- Use denylists (`deniedMcpServers`) to block specific servers (takes precedence)
- Enable approval for project MCP servers: `enableAllProjectMcpServers: true`

**Common Mistakes:**
- Committing sensitive API keys in `.mcp.json` (use env vars and secrets management)
- Mixing personal MCPs into project `.mcp.json` (pollutes team setup)
- Not reviewing MCP server permissions before adding

---

### 5. Skills

**How Skills Load:**
- Skills activate automatically when Claude detects relevance to the task
- Triggered explicitly with `/skill-name`
- Skills only load content when invoked, unlike CLAUDE.md (on-demand)
- Support YAML frontmatter for invocation control (user-only, auto, never)

**Best Practices:**
- Use skills for multi-step procedures, checklists, reference material
- Use CLAUDE.md for facts and rules that apply to every session
- Create skill when CLAUDE.md entry becomes a procedure (not a fact)
- Organize with `.claude/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md` + supporting files

**Common Mistakes:**
- Moving large reference material to CLAUDE.md instead of skills (wastes context)
- Ambiguous activation triggers (specify when/why Claude should invoke)
- Skills that don't include clear example usage or expected output format

---

### Summary: When to Use Each

| Use Case | Mechanism |
|----------|-----------|
| Build commands, must-know facts | CLAUDE.md |
| Growing learnings over time | Auto memory |
| Block dangerous commands | PreToolUse hook |
| Multi-step procedures, checklists | Skills |
| Team integrations (GitHub, DB) | `.mcp.json` |
| Personal preferences, tools | `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` + `~/.claude.json` |

**Key Rule:** Smaller, more specific CLAUDE.md → better adherence. Move bulk content to skills, rules, or auto memory.

content.thinking:
Excellent! The research gave me great information. Now let me compare what the docs recommend vs what the user has, and identify gaps.

**Key findings from research:**

1. **Exit codes**: exit 0 = no decision, exit 2 = blocking error. NOT exit 1! This means my pre-check command is wrong - I should use `exit 2` to block, not `exit 1`.

2. **Hook precedence**: 
   - Managed > `.claude/settings.local.json` > `.claude/settings.json` > `~/.claude/settings.json`
   - This means global settings have LOWEST priority, not highest!
   
3. **Local scope files**: `.claude.local.md` is gitignored for personal notes, and `~/.claude.json` handles personal MCPs across projects rather than project-level configs.

4. **Available hooks**: The docs mention SessionStart, PreToolUse, PostToolUse, and Stop - PreCompact doesn't appear in the standard list, so that might be custom or newer.

5. **Memory loading**: The first 200 lines or 25KB of MEMORY.md loads per session with individual topic files loaded on-demand, which aligns with our current approach.

Now I'm reviewing what we've implemented so far - lean CLAUDE.md files, global and project-level configs, auto memory via session recorder, PreToolUse for blocking dangerous commands, PostToolUse for validation, and skills for reference material. I need to fix the exit code handling in the pre-check command since the docs specify exit 0 means no decision and exit 2 means blocking error, so I need to clarify how PreToolUse interprets these codes. The MCPs should ideally live in `~/.claude.json` for global access across projects, but since that's not set up yet, keeping them in the project's `.mcp.json` works fine and ensures they're available when the template is copied to new projects. The distinction is that user-scope MCPs go global while team-required ones stay project-local, though for personal use either approach works.

I'm also noting that `claude.local.md` could be useful for sandbox-specific configurations, and the PreCompact hook seems to be working even though it wasn't in the standard documentation I found. Now I'm comparing my current setup against best practices to see where there are gaps—looking at how the global and project CLAUDE.md files align with recommendations, plus checking that the tool-use hooks are properly configured.

I've spotted one concrete issue: the exit code in the PostToolUse pre-check is using `exit 1` when it should be `exit 2` to properly signal a block. Moving MCPs to a global `~/.claude.json` would be cleaner but it's lower priority. Everything else looks solid, so I'll present these findings and make that exit code fix.

content.text:
---

## השוואה — מה שיש vs מה שמומלץ

| נושא | מומלץ רשמית | המצב שלך | פער |
|---|---|---|---|
| Global CLAUDE.md | העדפות אישיות, <200 שורות | Non-negotiables + environment + style | ✓ |
| Project CLAUDE.md | קצר, עובדות ספציפיות לפרויקט | 18 שורות + @rules | ✓ |
| PreToolUse | חסימת פקודות מסוכנות | ✓ גלובלי | ✓ |
| PostToolUse | ולידציה אחרי כתיבה | ✓ (typecheck + reviewer) | exit code תוקן |
| Stop | review + feedback | ✓ ריוי + session recorder | ✓ |
| Memory | MEMORY.md <200 שורות | index תקין | ✓ |
| Skills | on-demand בלבד | ✓ 500+ זמינים | ✓ |
| MCPs אישיים | `~/.claude.json` גלובלי | ב-project `.mcp.json` + template | ⚠️ (ראה הלן) |

---

**ממצא אחד שכדאי לדון בו:**

לפי התיעוד, MCPs *אישיים* (Playwright, GitHub, Context7) צריכים לגור ב-`~/.claude.json` ברמה גלובלית — לא ב-`.mcp.json` פר-פרויקט. כך הם זמינים בכל פרויקט בלי צורך בהעתקה.

רוצה שאעביר אותם לגלובלי, או שהגישה הנוכחית (template שמועתק) מספיקה לך?

- Remove or update the template .mcp.json (it should maybe just be a note about what MCPs to add)
- Now looking at what else the research recommended that we haven't implemented yet—most of the key improvements like lean CLAUDE.md files, on-demand skills, and PreToolUse blocking are already in place, so I should focus on whether the global CLAUDE.md needs to be split since it currently mixes personal and project-specific content.

- For MCPs, the research suggests moving them to `~/.claude.json` as the global config file, which would store user-scoped MCPs. I need to check if that file exists and what's currently in it, then decide whether to use the CLI command or edit it directly.

Writing config JSON... I'm realizing the `inputs` mechanism with `promptString` for storing secrets might work differently at the global config level. The safest approach is to use the `claude mcp add` CLI command, which handles the proper format and secret management automatically. For the GitHub MCP specifically, I need to figure out if the CLI supports environment variable flags or if there's a different way to pass the PAT token at the global level.

There's also a complication with Playwright's configuration—the current `.mcp.json` specifies project-specific paths like `.playwright-mcp/` for storage state. If I move Playwright to global config, these relative paths would still work since they'd be relative to the project root, but I need to verify that session persistence functions correctly in this setup.

I'm going to proceed with adding the MCPs globally using the `claude mcp add` command with the appropriate scope and arguments. I'm realizing that GitHub MCP needs the PAT through the promptString mechanism in `.mcp.json`, so it makes sense to keep that in the project template rather than moving it globally. I'll add Playwright and context7 to the global scope with `claude mcp add -s user`, then verify the setup works across new projects.

      "prompt": "You are helping a power user generate an onboarding guide for teammates who are new to Claude Code. The guide will live in the team's onboarding docs and can be pasted into Claude for an interactive walkthrough.\n\nYou're co-authoring this with them ג€” collaborative and helpful, like a teammate who's done this before and is happy to share.\n\n## Usage data (last {{WINDOW_DAYS}} days)\n\nThis was scanned from the guide creator's local Claude Code transcripts:\n\n```json\n{{USAGE_DATA}}\n```\n\n## Your task\n\nBefore anything else ג€” including before thinking through the classification ג€” output exactly this line as your first visible text:\n\n> Looking at how you've used Claude over the last {{WINDOW_DAYS}} days to put together an onboarding guide for teammates new to Claude Code.\n\nThis must come before any extended thinking about session descriptors. The guide creator is staring at a blank screen until you do. Classification is step 2, not step 1.\n\nGenerate the guide immediately, then ask for revisions. Don't wait for answers first ג€” it's easier for the guide creator to edit a concrete draft than answer abstract questions.\n\n1. **Output the acknowledgment line above.** No thinking, no classification, no tool calls before this. One line, then move on.\n\n2. **Derive the work-type breakdown.** Read the `sessionDescriptors` array ג€” each entry describes one session via its title, any linked code reviews (`prNumbers`), and first user message. Classify each session into one of these task types:\n\n   - **build_feature** ג€” new functionality, scripts, tools, config/CI/env setup\n   - **debug_fix** ג€” investigating and fixing bugs\n   - **improve_quality** ג€” refactoring, tests, cleanup, code review\n   - **analyze_data** ג€” queries, metrics, number crunching\n   - **plan_design** ג€” architecture, approach, strategy, understanding unfamiliar code, design review\n   - **prototype** ג€” spikes, POCs, throwaway exploration\n   - **write_docs** ג€” PRDs, RFCs, READMEs, design docs, copy/doc review\n\n   Categories describe the *type of task*, not the project or domain ג€” a teammate on any project should recognize them. Review sessions belong with whatever's being reviewed: code review is improve_quality, doc review is write_docs, design review is plan_design. Most sessions fit the list; only invent a new category if it's genuinely a different type of task. Pick the top 3-5 with rough percentages. First messages alone are usually enough; titles and code-review links are enrichment. If first messages are uninformative, use tool and MCP counts as a weak hint. If there are ~0 sessions, leave the breakdown as a TODO.\n\n   In the rendered guide, display categories with spaces and title case (e.g. \"Build Feature\" not \"build_feature\").\n\n3. **Gather the remaining pieces.** For repos, start with `currentRepo` and check the workspace for sibling repo directories. For MCP server setup, use each entry's `name` (and `urlOrigin` where present) to infer what the server does and how a teammate would get access. content.thinking:
The PostToolUse pre-check command exited with code 2 (blocking error) for the `.mcp.json` file write, which is the intended behavior! It's blocking the agent from running on non-code files. But the system is showing this as a "blocking error" message to the user.

The issue is that `exit 2` produces a "blocking error" which surfaces to the user as a visible message. This is noisy - the user sees an error every time a JSON file is written.

The fix: instead of `exit 2`, I should use a different mechanism. Looking at the research again: "exit 0 = no decision; exit 2 = blocking error". 
Actually, I'm realizing that PostToolUse hooks run after the tool executes, so they can't actually prevent the tool from running in the first place. And `exit 2` creates that noisy blocking error message the user sees. Maybe the better approach is to just remove the pre-check hook entirely and find a way to make the agent truly silent about these operations.

Looking at the bigger picture, I could either restructure how the global PostToolUse hook works or remove it altogether. The code reviewer doing real-time auto-fixes is creating noise when there's already code review happening elsewhere in the pipeline. Stripping out the global PostToolUse hook entirely might be the cleanest solution here. I'm deciding whether to remove just the pre-check command or both it and the agent entirely. The cleanest approach is to remove the global PostToolUse section completely since the project-level typecheck handles validation and the Stop hook covers code review—keeping it would just add unnecessary complexity.

3. Read the file content.
4. Scan for these issues and AUTO-FIX them if found:
   a. console.log / console.error / console.warn calls — remove them entirely
   b. Blocks of commented-out code (not explanatory comments, just dead code) — delete them
   c. TypeScript `any` type — replace with `unknown` or a proper type
   d. Obvious code duplication (3+ identical lines) — extract to a variable or helper
5. For these issues, DO NOT auto-fix — add a comment ONLY if there is no comment already:
   - Functions longer than ~30 lines (note: consider extracting)
   - Magic numbers used in logic (note: consider naming as a constant)
6. After each Edit you make, re-read the file to verify the fix looks correct and didn't introduce new issues.
7. Repeat the scan-and-fix loop until a full scan finds no more auto-fixable issues.
8. If you made no changes, do nothing and return silently.
